title: Sweet Potato Production Guide for Zimbabwe
content:
SWEET POTATO PRODUCTION IN ZIMBABWE

Sweet potato (Ipomoea batatas) is one of Zimbabwe's most important food security crops, particularly for smallholder farmers. It fills the critical 'hungry season' gap between main cereal harvests (December-February). Zimbabwe grows both white-fleshed and orange-fleshed varieties.

VARIETIES:
- Brondal: White-fleshed, most widely grown. Very drought tolerant. High dry matter (30-35%). Excellent for roasting. Yields 15-30 t/ha.
- Beauregard: Orange-fleshed (OFSP). Very high beta-carotene (Vitamin A). Sweet, moist texture. Yields 20-40 t/ha. Promoted for nutrition.
- Chingovha: Traditional white-fleshed. Extremely hardy. Very high dry matter (33-38%). Excellent in-ground storage. Yields 12-25 t/ha.
- Irene: Orange-fleshed from CIP. Good virus tolerance. Yields 18-35 t/ha.

ORANGE-FLESHED SWEET POTATO (OFSP) FOR NUTRITION:
OFSP varieties like Beauregard and Irene are critically important for addressing Vitamin A deficiency, which affects over 35% of children under 5 in Zimbabwe. A 125g serving of OFSP provides over 100% of daily Vitamin A needs. NGOs and government are actively promoting OFSP through vine distribution programmes.

SOIL AND SITE SELECTION:
- Prefers light, well-drained sandy loams. pH 5.5-6.5.
- Avoid heavy clays and waterlogged areas (causes rot and misshapen tubers).
- Tolerates poor fertility better than most crops.
- Good rotation crop after cereals (maize, sorghum).

LAND PREPARATION:
- Plough and harrow to fine tilth.
- Make ridges 30-40cm high, 90cm apart. Ridges are ESSENTIAL for good tuber development.
- On slopes, ridges should follow contour lines.

PLANTING MATERIAL:
- Propagated by vine cuttings, NOT seed.
- Use 30-40cm tip cuttings from healthy, virus-free mother plants.
- Never take cuttings from plants showing mosaic, stunting, or leaf curl (signs of SPVD).
- Vine Multiplication: Establish rapid multiplication plots 2-3 months before main planting. Irrigate if possible. One hectare requires approximately 33,000 cuttings.
- Source clean planting material from research stations (DR&SS, CIMMYT) or certified vine multipliers.

PLANTING:
- Plant October to December with first rains. Can plant up to January in wetter areas.
- Insert cuttings at 45-degree angle on top or side of ridge, burying 2/3 of the cutting (2-3 nodes underground).
- Spacing: 90cm between ridges, 25-30cm between plants.
- Firm soil around cuttings. Water if planting in dry conditions.

FERTILIZATION:
- Sweet potato is a low-input crop but responds to moderate fertilization.
- Basal: Compound D at 200-300 kg/ha at ridge making.
- AVOID excess nitrogen - causes excessive vine growth ('all leaf, no tuber').
- Potassium is the most important nutrient for tuber bulking. Apply Muriate of Potash (MOP) at 100 kg/ha if available.
- Organic matter/compost is ideal: 5-10 t/ha.
- Sweet potato extracts large amounts of potassium from soil. Rotate with cereals.

WATER MANAGEMENT:
- Rainfed: 500-750mm rainfall is sufficient.
- Irrigated: 25mm per week. Reduce irrigation 3-4 weeks before harvest to concentrate sugars.
- Drought response: Sweet potato can survive extended dry spells by shedding leaves and regrowing when rains return. This is a key food security advantage.

WEED MANAGEMENT:
- Weed 2-3 times before canopy closure.
- First weeding at 3-4 weeks after planting.
- Second weeding at 6-8 weeks, combined with earthing up.
- After canopy closure (8-10 weeks), the dense vine cover suppresses weeds naturally.

EARTHING UP:
- Earth up (re-ridge) at 6-8 weeks to cover exposed tubers.
- Prevents Sweet Potato Weevil access to tubers.
- Prevents greening of tubers from sun exposure.

title: Sweet Potato Pest and Disease Management in Zimbabwe
content:
SWEET POTATO PESTS AND DISEASES IN ZIMBABWE

PEST #1 - SWEET POTATO WEEVIL (Cylas puncticollis & Cylas brunneus):
The most destructive pest of sweet potato in Zimbabwe and across sub-Saharan Africa.

Identification: Small (6-8mm) dark blue-black weevil with long snout. Larvae are white, legless grubs found tunnelling inside tubers.

Damage: Adult females lay eggs in tubers and vine bases. Larvae bore into tubers creating tunnels, causing bitter taste (terpenoid compounds), and making tubers unmarketable. Can destroy entire harvest.

Management (Integrated Approach - NO single solution works alone):
1. CULTURAL CONTROLS (most important):
   - Plant early (October-November) to avoid dry-season population buildup
   - Earth up regularly to cover exposed tubers
   - Harvest on time - do NOT leave tubers in ground past maturity
   - Remove and destroy old crop residues and volunteer plants
   - Rotate fields (do not plant sweet potato in same field 2 years running)
   - Use clean, weevil-free planting material
   - Avoid cracking in ridges (weevils enter through cracks to reach tubers)
2. BIOLOGICAL CONTROL:
   - Entomopathogenic fungi (Beauveria bassiana) show promise in research
   - Natural predators include ants and parasitic wasps
3. CHEMICAL CONTROL:
   - Dipping vine cuttings in Chlorpyrifos before planting
   - NOT recommended to spray mature crop (pesticide residues in food crop)

PEST #2 - WHITEFLY (Bemisia tabaci):
- Transmits Sweet Potato Virus Disease (SPVD)
- Control: Use virus-free planting material. Remove infected plants.

PEST #3 - APHIDS:
- Transmit viruses. Feed on growing tips.
- Control: Usually not economically damaging. Natural predators (ladybirds) control them.

PEST #4 - MOLE RATS:
- Dig and eat tubers underground.
- Control: Trapping. Planting near homestead. Some farmers use vibrating devices.

DISEASE #1 - SWEET POTATO VIRUS DISEASE (SPVD):
The most important disease. Caused by co-infection of Sweet Potato Feathery Mottle Virus (SPFMV) and Sweet Potato Chlorotic Stunt Virus (SPCSV).

Symptoms: Severe leaf distortion, mosaic, stunting, chlorosis, leaf curling, tiny misshapen tubers. Yield losses of 80-90% in infected plants.

Management:
- Use virus-free planting material (MOST IMPORTANT)
- Source vines from certified multiplication plots
- Rogue (remove and destroy) infected plants immediately
- Do NOT take cuttings from infected plants
- Control whitefly vectors
- Some OFSP varieties (Irene) have moderate SPVD tolerance

DISEASE #2 - ALTERNARIA BLIGHT (Alternaria bataticola):
- Brown/black spots on leaves. Worse in wet weather.
- Control: Use disease-free planting material. Crop rotation.

DISEASE #3 - BLACK ROT (Ceratocystis fimbriata):
- Black, sunken lesions on tubers. Bitter taste.
- Control: Use clean planting material. Avoid wounding tubers at harvest. Cure properly.

DISEASE #4 - SCAB (Elsinoe batatas):
- Raised, corky lesions on stems and petioles.
- Control: Use resistant varieties. Rotate fields.

title: Sweet Potato Harvesting and Post-Harvest Handling in Zimbabwe
content:
SWEET POTATO HARVESTING AND POST-HARVEST

WHEN TO HARVEST:
- Harvest at 3.5-5 months after planting depending on variety.
- Signs of maturity: lower leaves yellowing, tuber size adequate (test dig 1-2 plants to check).
- Do NOT delay harvest - weevil damage increases dramatically in dry weather.
- For white-fleshed types (Brondal, Chingovha): can do piecemeal harvesting - dig tubers as needed over 2-3 months.
- For OFSP types (Beauregard, Irene): harvest at maturity - they do not store as well in-ground.

HOW TO HARVEST:
- Cut vines 2-3 days before harvest to make digging easier.
- Use a fork or hoe to gently lift tubers. Avoid cutting or bruising.
- Sweet potato skin is VERY fragile - handle like eggs.
- Sort in field: undamaged tubers for storage/market, damaged tubers for immediate consumption.

CURING (Critical Step Most Farmers Skip):
- Hold tubers at 25-30C and 85-90% relative humidity for 5-7 days.
- This heals harvest wounds, thickens skin, extends shelf life.
- Simple method: Stack tubers in shade, cover with grass/banana leaves to maintain humidity.
- Cured tubers store 3x longer than uncured tubers.

STORAGE:
- Sweet potato is perishable compared to cereals. Fresh tubers last 1-4 weeks at ambient temperature.
- With proper curing: 2-3 months at 13-15C, 85% humidity.
- Traditional pit storage: Dig pit 1m deep, line with grass, stack tubers, cover with soil and grass. Can store 2-3 months.
- Do NOT store below 12C (chilling injury) or above 20C (sprouting and decay).
- Do NOT store with chemicals or near petrol/diesel.

PROCESSING FOR LONGER STORAGE:
1. DRIED CHIPS/SLICES: Peel, slice into 3-5mm pieces, dry in sun for 2-3 days. Can store 6-12 months.
2. FLOUR: Dry chips, mill into flour. Use for porridge, baking (bread, buns, cakes). Mix 20-30% sweet potato flour with wheat flour.
3. OFSP PUREE: Cook, mash, can be used fresh in baking or frozen.
4. CHIPS/CRISPS: Commercial processing opportunity.

MARKET INFORMATION:
- Sweet potato has growing demand in Zimbabwe's urban markets.
- OFSP commands premium prices due to nutrition awareness.
- Peak prices: September-November (before new harvest, supply is lowest).
- Off-season production under irrigation commands premium prices.
- Grading: Grade by size and skin condition. Uniform, unblemished tubers fetch best prices.

ROTATION AND INTERCROPPING:
- Sweet potato is an excellent rotation crop after cereals (maize, sorghum, millet).
- Can be intercropped with maize (plant sweet potato between maize rows after maize tasselling).
- Fixes soil structure with extensive root system.
- Should not follow groundnuts or other root crops (builds up soil-borne diseases).
